title: Unified Framework for Functional Theories of Quantum Systems
authors: Chih-Chun Wang, Julia Liebert, Markus Penz, Christian Schilling
abstract: We introduce and study a unified framework for density-functional theory and its variants for quantum systems on finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces. These theories seek to reduce the complexity inherent in the many-body quantum problem by describing ground states through reduced variables. The central ingredients of our unified framework are a generalized choice of basic observables, whose expectation values define precisely those reduced variables, and a fixed part of the Hamiltonian characterizing the class of quantum systems under consideration. It is this minimal structure, which we call the scope of a functional theory, that is necessary and sufficient for the formulation of a functional theory. In particular, it allows one to define the universal functionals, establish their convexity and differentiability properties, address representability questions, and prove a Hohenberg-Kohn-type uniqueness result. A purification construction also relates ensemble and weighted-ensemble functionals to the pure-state variant. Particular emphasis is placed on functional theories with Lie-algebra observable structures, connecting the variational framework to symplectic geometry. The result of this work is a systematic mathematical formulation in which structural results can be proved once and applied across a broad class of finite-dimensional functional theories.
summary: This paper introduces a unified mathematical framework for functional theories of quantum many-body systems by reformulating the problem using reduced variables derived from basic observables. It systematically analyzes the relationship between pure-state and ensemble-state descriptions ($	ext{B}_p$ vs $	ext{B}_e$) across various models, providing powerful structural theorems applicable to strongly correlated electron systems.
main_problem: To develop a unified mathematical framework for density-functional theory (DFT) and its variants for quantum systems on finite-dimensional Hilbert spaces.
main_result: The work establishes that the structural properties of functional theories can be proven systematically once, applicable across a broad class of finite-dimensional models defined by their 'scope.'
method: The framework uses reduced variables (expectation values of basic observables) to replace the full quantum state description, employing tools from functional analysis and symplectic geometry.
model_or_system: The theory applies to general quantum systems, including lattice fermions (Hubbard model), spin systems (qubits), and bosons on periodic lattices. The scope is defined by a set of basic observables and a fixed Hamiltonian part.
key_observables: Reduced variables ($\mu$), ground-state energy functional $E(v)$, pure-state observable range ($	ext{B}_p$), and ensemble-state observable range ($	ext{B}_e$).
important_parameters: The dimension of the parameter space ($m=\dim V$) and the nature of interactions (e.g., repulsive vs. attractive $U$ in dimer models).
main_assumptions: The framework is most rigorous in the finite-dimensional setting, allowing for systematic mathematical proofs regarding representability.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the geometric relationship between $	ext{B}_p$ (e.g., two spheres) and $	ext{B}_e$ (the convex hull filling the gaps) for specific models like the double qubit.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the general scope $S=(V, H, \iota, H_0)$ to analyzing the geometric properties of observable ranges ($	ext{B}_p$ vs $	ext{B}_e$), and finally applying these structural results to specific physical examples like lattice fermions.
why_interesting: It provides a highly abstract yet powerful mathematical machinery that unifies several advanced concepts in condensed matter theory, such as DFT, RDMFT, and geometric constraints, which is crucial for understanding strongly correlated materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. The set Bp from the double qubit example consists of two Bloch spheres in (ρ1, ρ2, ρ3) at ρ4 = ±1 and all convex combinations between two points from each sphere. Leaving out the coordinate ρ3 in this illustration makes the spheres appear as circles on the top and bottom. Setting ρ3 = 0, the resulting shape is a cylinder from which two cones are removed at the top and bottom. Its convex hull, the set Be, is then the full cylinder.

Figure 2. The pure-state and ensemble observable ranges Bp = Be of the one-dimensional bosonic momentum-functional theory for lattice size L = 3, particle number N = 4, and total momentum p = 0, 1, 2. For p = 0, Bp = Be is colored in gray, and the set of critical values Bc excluding the relative boundary is indicated in black.

Figure 3. The pure-state constrained search is performed along the blue dashed line lying in the pure-state observable range Bp. In the ﬁgure, we only show the slice of Bp on which ⟨X1⟩and ⟨Y1⟩vanish.
Figure 4. Values of the pure-state constrained-search functional Fp and the ensemble constrained-search functional Fe at densities ρ = (0, 0, z, 0), with z ∈(−1, 0) ∪(0, 1) (see Fig. 3).
